title: Hierarchical Representations in RNNs
concept: Similar to CNN hierarchies, lower stacked RNN layers capture local word relations while deeper layers learn abstract sentence structures.

exercises.prompt: What does it mean to create a 'deep' or 'hierarchical' RNN?

options.text: Stacking multiple RNN layers on top of each other.
Using only one layer with no memory.
Deleting words from the sentence at random.

exercises.explain: Just like stacking blocks, stacking multiple recurrent layers on top of each other creates a deeper network that can learn more complex structures.

cards.title: Reading in Layers
cards.body: When you read a book, your eyes first read individual letters, then group them into words, and finally connect them to understand the plot. Did you know we can stack RNNs to process language in the exact same step-by-step way?

cards.title: What is Hierarchical Representation?
cards.body: Hierarchical representation in RNNs is the practice of stacking recurrent layers on top of each other. The bottom layers capture short-term, local relationships between nearby words, while the deeper layers process these patterns to understand overall sentence structure and meaning.

cards.title: The Newsroom Hierarchy
cards.body: Think of a newsroom. The frontline reporter writes down the exact quotes and spelling (like lower RNN layers focusing on nearby words). The chief editor reads the whole draft to understand the main message and tone (like deep RNN layers grasping the big-picture sentence structure).

cards.title: Tracking Long Sentences
cards.body: In the sentence 'The cat that chased the mice is sleeping', a lower RNN layer focuses on linking 'chased' to 'mice'. A deeper, stacked RNN layer keeps track of the main subject 'The cat' and connects it all the way to its action 'is sleeping'.

cards.title: Myth: One Layer Can Do It All
cards.body: You might think that a single RNN layer is enough to understand everything in a complex text. In reality, single layers often struggle with long-distance grammar. Stacking layers helps the network divide and conquer, with different levels focusing on different details.

cards.title: Layer vs. Layer
cards.body: Different layers in a stacked Recurrent Neural Network have different jobs when reading text.

left.text: Focus on local word transitions and short phrases (e.g., 'cat chased').

right.text: Focus on overall sentence structure and long-term meaning (e.g., subject-verb agreement).

exercises.prompt: What kind of information does the first (lowest) layer of a stacked RNN typically capture?

options.text: Local, nearby word relations and spelling patterns.
The overall abstract theme of an entire book.
Only the punctuation marks at the end of paragraphs.
Nothing; they are ignored by the network.

exercises.explain: Lower layers in a stacked RNN process the immediate input sequence, picking up on nearby word relationships and basic grammar patterns.

exercises.prompt: Stacked RNNs share a similar hierarchy concept to CNNs, moving from local details to abstract structures.
exercises.explain: Yes! Just like CNNs extract simple edges first and complex objects later, stacked RNNs extract simple word pairs first and complex sentence structures later.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the description of stacked RNN layers.
exercises.sentence: Lower RNN layers focus on local word patterns, while the _____ layers capture abstract sentence structure.
exercises.explain: Just like in CNNs, deeper stacked layers in an RNN handle the most abstract and high-level structural meanings, while lower layers handle immediate word relationships.

exercises.prompt: Which of these models is designed to build a hierarchical understanding of text?

options.text: A model that flattens all sentences into a single list of letters with no layers.
A stacked RNN where bottom layers track grammar rules and top layers track story themes.
A model that randomly shuffles words to guess the meaning of a sentence.

exercises.explain: The middle model is the AI because stacking RNN layers allows it to build a hierarchy of language patterns, going from simple local words to complex global structures.

exercises.prompt: If you stack multiple RNN layers on top of each other, how do their responsibilities change from the bottom layer to the top layer?

options.text: They discard sequential order entirely to speed up training.
Lower layers process local word sequences, while deeper layers capture abstract sentence-level meaning.
Deeper layers only memorize spelling while lower layers write the final summary.
They process words in a completely random order to prevent bias.

exercises.explain: Lower stacked RNN layers process immediate word transitions, while the higher stacked layers capture the overall context and sentence structure.
